sentences.text: My relationship with my father-- obviously, it can't be given in a straight definition, simply because I lived with him right up to the age of 20.

And the relationship of children and parents, and teenagers and parents, and then grown up and parents-- especially when the-- the-- my generation compared with his generation had made such strides in terms of attitudes toward so many things, and to life in general, and to Jewish life in particular.
Obviously, it had its points of-- of disagreement, and points of argument, and so on, which-- part of the spice of life, as far as my personal life is concerned-- [COUGHS] excuse me.

In the-- he sent me to different primary [NON-ENGLISH], a sort of a full-time day schools-- primary day schools, which in those days meant from 9:00 in the morning till 4:00 or 5:00 in the afternoon at least.

And I had one school where I lived-- where I stayed till 7:00 in the evening, when-- in the winter time, in the Bialystok when it was dark, we used to walk home with lanterns in our hands, not so much really for the need of it, but for the quaintness of it, for children.

I remember making out of a tin of [? spreads, ?] making holes in it and put a candle in it, because batteries and these sort of things was-- if they were available, they weren't available to everybody.
And so just to give an idea of the time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] span, in retrospect.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the beginning, of course, I was very-- a very-- cooperative, in terms of-- apart from the childish tantrums and childish-- in fact, I was sometimes rebellious because my father wouldn't wake me up at 12:00 at night to go to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He let me sleep.
He had [INAUDIBLE] and didn't wake me up.
And I made scandals for overlooking me and treating me as a kid, when I felt I already had to be part of the congregations and so on-- the ambitions of a young boy and so on.
And of course, the bar mitzvah was still observed, with all the formalities, although not in the way it's done here.
I didn't have to have teachers to teach me the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I didn't have to learn by heart, things like that.
And when-- and I made my first speech at afternoon table in [INAUDIBLE], without having written out on a piece of paper, and so on, in the presence of the headmaster of the school and the classes.
But then, at the age of 14, after putting on [INAUDIBLE] every morning, religiously, I rebelled.
It was enough.
By that time, I went-- I left and I went into gymnasium, which was an institution of a different nature.
And it was already in keeping with my understanding of what I wanted to be, what I aimed to be, and so on.
And one of the first things that went was my attendance on Shabbat morning in shul.
I stopped going with Father to shul, which was a terrible blow to him.
It was a terrible.
I understood that it was a terrible thing.
But I am not quite sure-- I can't put an exact date on it, whether it was 14, or 14 and 1/2, or whatever it is.
But that was the time of the break, in terms-- and he knew that the gap between us is-- is widening.
And then, at Shabbat table, we used to argue about the Jewish religion, and Jewish mannerisms, and God.
And yes, there is a God.

No, there isn't a God, and all the things that my generation's people were facing, facing the one generation before them argued, as some people probably do today, but maybe much more understandably so, being more exposed to TV, and to literature, and so on.
In those days, one had to be more-- more rebellious by nature to take on your own father with respect, respectfully.

But yet, at the same time, to agree to disagree, so to speak, in terms of such fundamentals, because we didn't talk about trivia-- we talked about the most fundamental issue in our very existence and so on.
But I would have never, never dreamed of speaking-- of using a world of dubious quality or respect in addressing my father or my mother.
It was always with the utmost respect.

It was so ingrained and it was so part of the culture of-- the nature-- the issue of [NON-ENGLISH], the respect-- respect your father and your mother, that it was almost like with your mother's milk, you had it.
And it is-- it was inconceivable that-- today you hear children speak to their parents.
It is-- I just-- I can't-- by now, yeah, I'm used to it.
But in the beginning, it was just, just-- I couldn't adjust to it.
I can't adjust to it.
So that was it.
But-- and in time, in time, although he would never compromise on these convictions in a religious sense, and so on, he understood.
He was sufficiently progressive to understand that he can't hold the tide of time back.
And he graciously-- outwardly, at least, if not inwardly-- gave in, sometimes with a smile and sometimes with a concerned brow that, my god, what are the times doing to me-- this sort of expression.

But just the same, it was a very warm, warm relationship, although there was no hugging, and there was no kissing, and there was no-- not sort of this outward manifestation of a dilettante sort of a attachment.
But it was deep, inward, tangible, yet undemonstrative, undemonstrative.

Well, it had the impact on my education because, in a sense, his insistence on giving me a Jewish education, to start with, gave me the grounding for everything else that I learned afterwards, because I-- by the time I have studied at the different schools-- before I get-- came to the gymnasium, I had so much, so much Hebrew knowledge and Talmudic knowledge, already attained, that I remember one of the first lessons, that I came to the class.
And the teacher, in Hebrew, which was a very brilliant teacher, discussed a certain item.
And he wanted some sort of a-- a form of expression to meet a certain concept.
And I lifted my finger up, and I quoted to him from [NON-ENGLISH], not from the Talmud text, but from [NON-ENGLISH], in Aramaic, which was just, just exactly what he looked for.
The man looked at me, and he said, yes.
He said, that's it.
He said, that's it.
If you've got that up your sleeve, then-- I remember when he took me into the class-- the same teacher-- he gave me a piece of paper.
And he asked me to write on a certain subject.
I don't remember what it was.
I wrote two sentences.
He came up, looked at the two sentences, and said, that will be enough.
That will be enough.
The only other thing that comes to mind is a scene that my father went, once, for a parent-teacher meeting.
And he came home, and he was beaming.
I've never seen him that-- he always was very serious looking.
He was all smiles and all satisfaction.
I said, Dad, what happened?
What happened?
He said, well, I don't know whether I should tell you that or not.
He said, but I will tell you, just the same.
He said, I was walking along the corridor, and suddenly I hear somebody calling my name from the back.
I turned around.
It was your Hebrew teacher, Moshe Zabludowski.
Of And he came up to me, and he spoke to me in Hebrew.
And he says to me in Hebrew, are you the father of Israel Kipen?
And he said, yes, I am.
And he quoted-- and he, in turn, said to my father in Hebrew a quotation from the Bible, [SPEAKING HEBREW], like him should multiply in Israel.
And when a father hears a teacher give such a sentence or opinion about a son, [? oh, ?] he was happy, obviously.
A million dollars wouldn't have made him smile and be-- to give him the pride and the expression.

I could see in his eyes that it meant all our arguments-- there is God, there is no God, or whatever it is-- didn't come into play for these three words that he heard from a teacher, spoken unprovoked and un-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming so freely.
And that is just one aspect of the-- that comes to mind, now that we are looking into the-- into the memory lane.
Yes, of course-- my mother-- my mother was a very presentable woman, as long as I remember her.
She must have been-- I remember a picture of her, hanging on the wall of her brother, where my grandfather lived.
It was a very beautiful picture of a very lovely young woman.
She must have been 17, 18 at the time-- a really beautiful young woman.
She had the misfortune of losing her mother very early.
And she was bought up by an uncle in Bialystok.
So she was an orphan, in terms of-- [INAUDIBLE], 17 or 18, whatever it is.
She was born in a small township, [NON-ENGLISH], which is not far from Bialystok.
And in those little townships, there may not have been too much opportunity of earning a living or having three sufficient meals a day.
But that was made up with a great deal of intellectual curiosity and learning, to fill the days and fill the inquisitiveness of mind.
And in that little township, she learned-- she spoke fluent Hebrew.
And she told me that she used to get, [INAUDIBLE], Hebrew periodicals.
She didn't have much money.
That was obvious, the situation was.
But the Hebrew periodicals [INAUDIBLE] instead of buying yourself a bit of powder for her face, she bought a periodical instead.
And at the age of 80, she wrote letters in Hebrew to relatives in Israel that amazed me then, because I, today-- and I-- my Hebrew was much deeper and much far-fetched.
I have to go to a dictionary today, to make sure the spelling of a word.
She didn't have to go to a dictionary, to such a degree.
She was, yes.
She was a religious also, in the sense, of course, that if she wouldn't have been religious, there would have been no match-- put it this way.
Yes, my father wouldn't have contemplated marrying a woman who was not religious.
Her father, as I told you, was a Talmudic scholar also, but not of the same level of rabbinical excellence that our-- of father's family-- different level, but rabbis.
And-- but she was a much more modern person.
If she would have married another type of man, she would have most probably been a different type of a lady, with much-- she loved speaking.
She would, at the drop of a pin, make a speech, unprepared, unrehearsed, and so on.
And she loved it-- and fluent, and so on.
Whatever the reason may be, [? most probably ?] [INAUDIBLE], credit to my mother, not my father.
My father was a man of few words, of very few words.
With my mother-- well, you know, mothers-- she was a mother of five children, and I was the oldest.
So the oldest child, according to Jewish law, gets an extra portion of everything, even-- even of love.
And-- and of course, I had-- I was looked on as somebody with some promise, although, as time moved on, my younger-- my other siblings were equally capable, by and large.
But it was a very warm, a very warm mother.
And she, if I got into trouble with Father, she would defend me.
She would stand [INAUDIBLE] to take my side, as any mother would do, and so on.
But it was a very lovely and very, very warm and cordial relationship, insofar-- and again, insofar that it was socially permissible to express these relations, even between parents and children.
It had to be at a safe distance.
Yes.
Yes, sure-- my-- next to me was my sister Shifra.
She was-- she was born two years [? after ?] [? me, ?] in '21.
That-- then was a brother, by the name Hershl, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] born two years after that.
And then was a brother, by the name Lazar, born in '25.
And the youngest, Simcha or Sam, as we know him here, born in '27.
All of them survived the war.
My parents and the five-- and the four children survived the war by the-- by the good grace of the Russian authorities send them to Siberia.
Otherwise, they would have not survived it.
Well, with my sister, I was at school.
And I lost a year coming from the other, lower schools.
And so I had to be in the same class with her, which was a bit-- in fact, I was in the same class with her and with my cousin, my uncle's only daughter.
So there were three Kipens in the same class.
And-- [COUGHS] but of course, being a girl and the only girl among four brothers, she held out a position of-- of separate identity and so on.
The younger brothers, of course, were already a bit too young when I was-- when I was a boy of 14, 15, they were still young children.
And then, before we knew, they went away.
And I was 20, and the youngest one was 12.
And they were sent to Siberia.
By the time they came back, we hardly knew one another, because the intervening war years-- we had different experiences, you see.
And it was a different background altogether.
The home as we knew it, and the rules, and the concepts that governed our life all went out through the window.
Yeah.

Yes, yes, well, I had-- I have managed too, and I use the word managed advisedly, to graduate from gymnasium [? matric, ?] even though I was taken out the year 11 for a half a year, on Father's request, for economic reasons of this business, and again in year 12 for part of the first thought third of the year.

I've-- despite of all that, I have done my [? matric ?] reasonably well and subsequently went forward to Warsaw, to the university in Warsaw, which was not a simple matter, because in the pre-war Poland, universities-- well, certain faculties were totally closed to Jewish students.
You couldn't get in medicine.
You couldn't get in law.
You couldn't get in on many of those.

The only-- the only chance that we had at all was to go to the arts faculty, because that was not considered to be, in terms of economic potential, of any particular value, and just to-- most probably not to-- not to have a reputation in the international community that the [INAUDIBLE] is applicable totally to-- [INAUDIBLE] entire university.
We were let in on the-- in the faculty of arts.
But one-- a major aspect of it has to be accentuated and recorded.
When I picked up my index-- because every student in Europe has an index.
When I picked up my index with the photograph on the left inside page, underneath my photograph was a rubber stamp.
And that stamp said that my place is reserved on the left side of the lecture hall.
In other words, by law of the university, we were not allowed to sit on the same benches as our Polish colleagues.
So it was a ghetto within the university classroom.
Jewish students who would not submit to that rule used to stand at the back wall, supporting the wall, and taking notes standing up, standing up.
I had the temerity, or the folly, one day to come into a class and sit on the right side of the hall, defying [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the regulations.
A youthful folly is [INAUDIBLE] is doing all sorts of things.

And I was told later on, because I went to talk to the professor, somebody came in and told me to try and stay and engage the man for as long as I can, because outside on the stairs, the students are standing on both sides of the staircase, waiting for me to come down, to beat me up.
And much to my delight, the professor was so responsive, the more questions I threw at him, the more he got involved.
And they got sick and tired of waiting for me, and they dispersed.
And by the time I came out, there was nobody there.

The reaction was not a-- it was very chilling, but not altogether unexpected, because I remember that every time I used to walk through the iron gate of the university campus of Warsaw, which was at the most beautiful part of Warsaw.
It was a magnificent city, a magnificent European city.
Every time you walked through that gate, any Jewish student was mentally prepared to get bowed over, to get a hit, even-- even a knife, even a knife.
I-- my experience was-- my experience was that we had to get a certain number of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] signatures from professors to just-- to prove that we attended lectures.
So we had to submit our indexes in a certain number of lectures so that the professor could sign his name and have a record that we have attended.
But not in all-- not in all-- I had to go, once, to get a signature from a professor in the law school.
And that was a no-no.
We are not-- we were not allowed in.
Normally, Jewish students used to hand over their index to the [? man ?] on the door.
And it was a business.
He used to get the signatures for them, and they used to give him zloty, two zlotys, whatever it is.
And that was it.
I smuggled in, and I put my thing in.
It was an experience.
And by the time I walked out, he noticed me.
And he looked at me in bewilderment.
How is it that you managed to evade me, and you came in?
And if anything-- if he could, he would have torn me to bits for that.
Never mind the zloty or two that he has lost.
That would have been his profit.
But-- [INAUDIBLE].
But I had-- my experience, in terms of physical health, was already, during the war, at the [?
Vilna ?] university, where I was coming out in the morning from lectures-- and without me realizing, that was the day of the pogrom.
It was a pogrom, if you know, and the interregnum between the Polish people leaving and the Lithuanian people just arriving.
And the minute they spotted me, they recognized that I am Jewish.
They threw me to the floor, and they were kicking me with their-- with their feet and hurting me.
By the time-- by the time a policeman came up to, like, have a look at the situation, my face was like that.
I took refuge in one of the houses there, and I didn't dare come out for hours, because-- until the street emptied out.

That was my personal experience, and it took me a couple of-- a couple of months before the swelling and the blue, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under my eyes, has disappeared sufficiently to be able not to have to wear glasses.
That was [? '94. ?]
I lived with a family of Hasidic Jews, also in the heart of town.
And it was a curious insight into life there, in the sense that, of course, the Warsaw Jew was, by and large, Hasidic.
But their children were already, if not assimilated, at least certainly not Hasidic.
And whilst-- when Father and Mother want to talk between themselves without their children understand what-- they used to talk Yiddish to one another.
And that was already a foreign language for their own children, although they were Hasidic themselves, to such an extent the gap between just one generation has been-- I didn't stay long.
I didn't [INAUDIBLE] also, because, again, if I had to submit to pressures at secondary school in year 11 and 12, how much more it was the case afterwards.
And after awhile, I decided, well, I've had a taste of that.
The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] war situation became more aggravated.
Everybody knew that the clouds, the war clouds, are gathering.
And it was on the-- it wasn't a question if.
The question was when it will break out.
I returned to Bialystok, I suppose-- I don't know, the beginning of '39 or late in '38, because I finished in '37.
Well, my ambitions-- of course, I was-- I was inclined to learn.

But they were tempered with the reality of life, and not only the reality of life, as far as the family is concerned, but also with the reality of life in-- in Poland and in the world, because no one, at the age of 19, could close his eyes and say the whole world doesn't see us.
We knew that our youth is going to be challenged.
Our lives are going to be broken any day now.

And in fact, for the last few years of that-- of that in our adolescent and early manhood, we never knew what youthful carelessness and freedom is, in this sense, because the weight of events and the weight of the situation-- the daily, the daily radio, the rantings and ravings of Hitler, and the things that were going on were part of our daily existence.
We waited for it, because we had to-- we had to understand and to appreciate how much closer we are getting to the moment of truth, in terms of unavoidable development of events.
And of course, Poland was the first victim.
The war started on the 1st of September, '39, in Poland.
